common_name: Petra Croton (Classic Multi-Color Bold-Foliage Tropical Shrub)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; maximum color intensity in Petra requires high light — move to more shade only to protect from extreme summer heat, not as a routine growing position.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Petra is the most widely planted croton (Codiaeum variegatum) cultivar in the United States — the standard by which other crotons are measured, producing large, boldly lobed, leathery leaves with spectacular multi-color variegation in green, yellow, orange, and bright red that develops most intensely in high light. A single leaf may display all four colors simultaneously, with yellow at the midrib transitioning through orange to red at the leaf margins and green background — creating the kind of botanical fireworks impossible to achieve with any other commonly available ornamental. 'Petra' is named for its large, broadly lobed leaf form with relatively uniform multicolor patterning, distinguishing it from more narrowly-leaved or sparsely variegated croton selections. In zone 9, Petra is widely used as a warm-season container plant for patios and entryways, providing dramatic tropical foliage from April through October before being moved indoors to overwinter in a bright window or warm greenhouse. In zone 10-12, Petra grows as an evergreen perennial shrub to 4–6 feet with permanent outdoor planting. The spectacular foliage combines effectively with solid-color tropicals — red ginger, golden canna, purple confederate rose — where the multi-color leaves tie together the surrounding single-color plants.

common_name: Mammy Croton (Dark Twisted-Leaf Red-and-Gold Croton Cultivar)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Mammy's strongly twisted and contorted leaf form is a defining ornamental characteristic — the spiral, corkscrew-shaped leaves distinguish it from all other commonly grown croton cultivars.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Mammy Croton is one of the most distinctive and recognizable croton cultivars — immediately identifiable by its strongly twisted, spiraling, contorted leaves that curl and spiral along their length rather than lying flat, creating an unusual three-dimensional texture unlike any other commonly available ornamental foliage plant. The leaf color is darker and more intensely red-dominated than Petra: deep, burgundy-red to red-and-gold coloring on a dark green base, with the red tones more predominant and the yellow more restricted to the midrib and splashes near the veins. The overall effect is a darker, moodier palette than the brighter, more orange-dominated Petra — making Mammy especially effective in tropical designs with other dark-leaved companions like black elephant ear, purple ti plant, and dark caladiums. In zone 9, Mammy is typically used as a warm-season container plant or annual-style tropical that is moved indoors before frost; in zone 10b-12 it grows as a permanent perennial shrub. The twisted leaf form is most pronounced in high light conditions — leaves in deep shade tend to become more flattened and less dramatically spiral. The unusual form makes Mammy Croton a striking conversation-piece plant in tropical container arrangements.

common_name: Blackie Sweet Potato Vine (Deep Purple-Black Ornamental Sweet Potato Vine)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants after the last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; 'Blackie' produces the most intense dark-purple coloring in full sun — plants in shade become more green-purple and lose the dramatic near-black color that is the primary ornamental feature.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants after the last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Blackie Sweet Potato Vine is a cultivar of ornamental sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas) selected for its deep purple-black to near-black leaf color — the most dramatic dark foliage of any commonly grown ornamental annual, producing a cascade of deeply lobed, star-shaped leaves in a color so dark it reads as nearly black in high contrast compositions with chartreuse or white companions. The purple-black color is produced by extremely high anthocyanin concentrations in the leaf tissue — the same pigment chemistry that makes purple basil, dark caladiums, and black elephant ear dark, but at an intensity that produces the closest approach to true black available in an annual foliage plant. 'Blackie' is one of the most vigorous ornamental sweet potato vines, spreading 4–8 feet during the Gulf South growing season to create a dense, ground-covering mat of dark foliage. The vine is one of the most effective design tools for creating contrast in mixed container plantings and annual border compositions: the deep purple-black creates dramatic negative space against which brighter flowers and lighter foliage appear to glow. In zone 9, Blackie grows as a warm-season annual, though the storage tubers sometimes survive mild winters in protected positions. Outstanding paired with 'Margarita' (chartreuse sweet potato vine), 'Diamond Frost' euphorbia, or bright orange and yellow tropical flowers.

common_name: Margarita Sweet Potato Vine (Brilliant Chartreuse Ornamental Sweet Potato Vine)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants after the last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; 'Margarita' maintains its chartreuse-yellow color in more shade than many other chartreuse foliage plants — it tolerates half-day shade while still providing good color, though full sun produces the most brilliant lime-yellow tones.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants after the last frost in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Margarita Sweet Potato Vine is a cultivar of ornamental sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas) producing large, heart-shaped leaves in brilliant chartreuse to lime-green-yellow — one of the most luminous and versatile foliage colors available in an annual, capable of brightening dark garden areas and creating long-distance visual impact with its pure lime-yellow intensity. The heart-shaped leaves are larger and less lobed than 'Blackie', creating a softer, more flowing texture that contrasts well with both fine-textured annuals and bold-leaved tropical companions. 'Margarita' and 'Blackie' are the classic pairing in container and border compositions — the chartreuse and near-black create one of the most dramatic foliage contrasts available from annual plants, each color intensifying the visual impact of the other. The vigorous trailing habit (4–8 foot spread during Gulf South warm season) makes Margarita outstanding as a groundcover annual, as a trailer over pot edges and retaining walls, and as a weed-suppressing annual between permanent plantings. The chartreuse color illuminates shaded border edges and creates visual warmth in combination with cool blue-purple or coral-pink flowers. In Gulf South summer heat, Margarita vines grow rapidly enough to cover significant ground in 6–8 weeks from transplanting.

common_name: Chuck Hayes Gardenia (Cold-Hardy Reblooming Double-White Gardenia)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 5.0–6.0) soil in partial shade; gardenias are notoriously pH-sensitive — yellow leaves (chlorosis) in established plants is the primary symptom of insufficient soil acidity; acidify with sulfur and use fertilizers formulated for acid-loving plants.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic (pH 5.0–6.0) soil in partial shade to morning sun with afternoon shade; Chuck Hayes is a standout gardenia cultivar selected for two key improvements over standard Gardenia jasminoides: superior cold-hardiness (zone 7 reliable, versus zone 8 for most gardenias) and a rebloom tendency that produces flowers in both spring and fall rather than the single spring-only bloom flush of the species. Named for the North Carolina gardener who selected and propagated it, Chuck Hayes represents a significant horticultural improvement that extends gardenia culture northward and provides a longer bloom season throughout the Southeast. The flowers are classic double-white gardenia — the iconic, deeply fragrant, creamy white multi-petaled blooms that represent the pinnacle of floral fragrance in the American garden — produced in April–June and again in September–October in favorable conditions. The compact, rounded habit (3–5 feet) is more manageable than standard gardenia, fitting typical residential border spacing without crowding. The cold-hardiness improvement makes Chuck Hayes the preferred choice for zone 7b-8a gardeners who have lost standard gardenias in severe winters, and provides additional security for zone 9 gardeners in the rare extreme cold events that affect the Gulf South. Gardenias require consistent moisture and strongly acidic soil — iron chlorosis from insufficiently acidic soil is the most common cultural problem.
